characters.title: Umm Yusra
characters.summary: A central character and a key figure in the story 'Umm Yusra'.
characters.body: Umm Yusra is the antagonist, the love interest, and the deity of the story, all in one. She is an absolute force who orchestrates Ziyad's transformation with terrifying precision.

characters.title: Ziyad al-Bani
characters.summary: Main protagonist of story 'Umm Yusra' and the central figure of the second story chain.
characters.body: Ziyad al-Bani is the main protagonist of the story 'Umm Yusra' and the central figure of the second story chain.

stories.title: Umm Yusra
stories.summary: This story is a piece of erotic fiction with strong themes of BDSM (Dominance and Submission), psychological transformation, and spiritual re-engineering. It tells the story of Ziyad, a timid young man in a speculative, gender-inverted matriarchal society, who is chosen by the powerful, enigmatic, and god-like figure, Umm Yusra.
stories.body:
Chapter 1: The Weight of Dust and Duty
Chapter Text
Iyed woke up to the sound of the morning call to prayer echoing through the old clay walls of his family’s narrow apartment. The sound wasn’t just spiritual—it was disciplinary. In this society, Fajr wasn’t a blessing; it was an obligation soaked in eyes that watched, judged, and measured every move.

His father’s voice soon followed, harsh and sharp like gravel under a boot:

“You’re not praying? Is that what you’re becoming? A mutiny of flesh?”

Iyed scrambled out of his thin mattress on the floor, wiping sleep from his face. He was twenty-one, but he felt twelve under his father’s gaze. The walls were close and uneven, filled with old religious calligraphy and women’s voices beyond thin walls—his mother, his sisters, his aunts—all of them taller, stronger, and louder than him. In this matriarchal society, a man wasn’t head of the house. A man was the house cat.

Men were not born for leadership, but for obedience. This had been drilled into him since he could walk. Women, especially older women, were the judges, the soldiers, the clerics, the business owners. Men were trained to serve, to remain modest, to be meek and God-fearing. It wasn’t enough to be pious—you had to be pliable.

At 5’8”, Iyed was an average man. But in a world where the average woman towered at 7 feet, he was small, feminine-looking, and delicate in build. His hands were soft. His arms were thin. His eyes were always lowered. And his voice rarely rose above a whisper.

The Streets Outside

After prayer and a cold breakfast his mother barely acknowledged, Iyed stepped out into the neighborhood, a place where everything was built for women. Doors were tall. Benches were wide. Adornments and surveillance cameras watched for male disobedience. Giant posters of niqab-clad women in positions of power decorated every wall:

“THE MOTHER KNOWS.”
“THE UTERUS COMMANDS.”
“A WOMAN IS CLOSER TO GOD.”

He passed by the community masjid—not allowed inside except during specific hours for male supplication. Outside, massive women, cloaked in niqabs and flowing garments, towered over the men walking two steps behind them. Most of them carried staffs or tasbih beads the size of his wrists. Even their perfume was an oppressive fog—sweet, heady, almost drugging.

Some of the women looked down at him as he passed, eyes dark and impassive behind the veil. One murmured:

“How sad. That one looks so… hollow. As if his own masculinity abandoned him.”

Home Life

Iyed had one brother—now gone, taken into the Domestic Training Corps, a euphemism for what was essentially spiritual re-education for men who refused to submit to marriage arrangements.

His mother, Sabira, was a towering woman, a retired state judge. Cold, calculating, devout. She didn’t hit him—but her silence was worse than violence.

His father, Khalid, was… hollow. A shell of a man who simply recited Qur’an and served tea. It wasn’t shameful—serving was virtuous. But there was no trace of fire left in the old man.

“Don’t make trouble, Iyed,” his father once said softly.
“If a woman chooses you, say yes. Even if it’s a punishment.”

Iyed’s Inner World

At night, Iyed retreated into his mind. He had no friends. No freedom. Just forbidden thoughts.

Sometimes he dreamed of being stepped on—not in pain, but in awe. Of being held down by a niqabi woman who smelled of jasmine and power.
He dreamed of veiled giants smothering him, not violently—but in a kind of holy ecstasy.
He hated these dreams.

Each morning, he repented.

Each night, they came again.

The Letter

One day, a letter arrived. His mother held it in her massive, ringed hand, her eyes unreadable.

“You’ve been chosen.”

He stared at her.

“By who…?”

“Umm Yusra. You’ve heard of her. She’s a scholar. A mystic. A pillar of the masjid. And she has asked for you.”

The room tilted. His stomach turned cold.

“But I—why me? I’m nothing.”

“Exactly,” his father whispered.

His mother continued, "She is **forty. Unmarried. Towering. Wealthy. Pious. Beautiful. Dominant. And she has selected you. You will accept this as qadr. God’s will."

“B-but… she’s… she’s like a queen.”

“And you are clay. Be shaped.”
